transformation_type: skeletal rearrangement of α-halo ketones to acids or ring-contracted carbonyl compounds
mechanism_type: nucleophilic addition to a non-enolizable α-halo ketone followed by semibenzilic-type 1,2-shift and halide displacement
reactant_pattern_text: α-halo ketone lacking α-hydrogen or bridgehead bicyclic α-halo ketone under basic or nucleophilic conditions
product_pattern_text: carboxylic acid derivative, ring-contracted ketone, or cyclic/bicyclic homologated carbonyl compound
common_conditions: typically initiated from isolated α-halo ketones or α-bromo alcohol precursors under strongly basic or nucleophilic conditions, often after cycloaddition or annulation steps
synonym_names: semibenzilic-type rearrangement of non-enolizable α-halo ketones
description_short: Favorskii-like rearrangement manifold for α-halo ketones that cannot enolize, delivering carboxylic acid derivatives or ring-contracted products through a semibenzilic pathway.

transformation_type: olefination of α-halosulfones to alkenes and ring-contracted cyclic alkenes
mechanism_type: base-induced deprotonation and intramolecular displacement to an episulfone followed by SO2 extrusion
reactant_pattern_text: α-halogenated sulfone treated with base or heat, optionally after in situ chlorination
product_pattern_text: acyclic alkene or ring-contracted cyclic alkene
common_conditions: strong base generates episulfone intermediates; solvent and temperature control alkene geometry and efficiency of SO2 extrusion
description_short: General olefination strategy that converts α-halosulfones into alkenes, often enabling convergent C=C installation or ring contraction from cyclic sulfones.

transformation_type: metal-mediated coupling of α-halo esters with carbonyl compounds
mechanism_type: organozinc or related metal enolate formation followed by aldol-type addition through a chairlike transition state
reactant_pattern_text: α-halo ester or α-halo carbonyl compound + aldehyde or ketone under Zn or metal-salt activation
product_pattern_text: β-hydroxy ester or, in the Blaise variant after work-up, β-keto ester
common_conditions: activated metal is used in ether-type solvents or under mild reductive conditions, and work-up releases β-hydroxy ester products from metal enolates
description_short: Classic zinc-based carbon–carbon bond-forming reaction between α-halo esters and carbonyl compounds that delivers β-hydroxy esters with broad modern metal-mediated variants.

transformation_type: transfer of diazo functionality to activated methylene compounds and α-formyl ketones
mechanism_type: base-promoted enolate addition to a sulfonyl azide, triazene formation, and fragmentation to α-diazo carbonyl compounds
reactant_pattern_text: active methylene β-dicarbonyl compound or α-formyl ketone + sulfonyl azide under basic conditions
common_conditions: requires sufficiently acidic substrates or deformylative activation of α-formyl ketones, usually with amine base and controlled addition of the diazo donor
description_short: Widely used diazo-transfer protocol that converts activated carbonyl compounds into α-diazo intermediates for Wolff rearrangements, cyclopropanations, and metal-carbene chemistry.

transformation_type: ortho formylation of phenols and heteroarenes via dichlorocarbene chemistry
mechanism_type: base-induced dichlorocarbene generation from chloroform followed by electrophilic addition and hydrolytic unveiling of the aldehyde
reactant_pattern_text: phenol or pyrrole treated with chloroform or another dichlorocarbene precursor under strongly basic aqueous conditions
product_pattern_text: ortho-formyl phenol, formylated pyrrole, or abnormal dichloromethyl cyclohexadienone/3-chloropyridine product
common_conditions: typically uses excess alkali and chloroform with warming; product class depends on substrate type and availability of abnormal ring-expansion pathways
description_short: Foundational dichlorocarbene-based formylation method for phenols and related heteroarenes that usually delivers ortho-aldehydes but can also produce abnormal rearrangement products.
